theory.intro: One linear relationship can appear as a **pattern**, a **table of values**, an **equation**, a **set of coordinates**, and a **straight-line graph**. All five describe the same pairs of $x$- and $y$-values, so you can move between them and check that they agree.
facts: A table column with $x=a$ and $y=b$ is the coordinate $(a,b)$, and the same pair is one point on the graph.
Representations of the same relationship give the same $y$-value for every shared $x$-value.
To decide whether two representations match, compare the $y$-values they give at the same $x$.
One matching point is not enough: two different lines can still cross at a single shared point.

foundation.question_text: Write each column of the table as a coordinate $(x,y)$. $\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&0&1&2&3\\\hline y&2&5&8&11\end{array}$

The table $\begin{array}{c|ccc}x&0&1&2\\\hline y&1&4&7\end{array}$ describes a relationship. Which coordinate set describes the same relationship: $A=\{(0,1),(1,4),(2,7)\}$ or $B=\{(1,0),(4,1),(7,2)\}$?

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=4,ymin=-3,ymax=8,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4},ytick={-2,0,2,4,6,8},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.3:3.6,samples=2] {3*x-2};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,-2) (1,1) (2,4) (3,7)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph shows a linear relationship. Is $(2,4)$ one of its points?
foundation.solution_text:
At $x=2$, the graph shows $y=4$.
Yes, $(2,4)$ is a point on the line.

foundation.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-1,ymax=7,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.7:4.3,samples=2] {x+3};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,3) (1,4) (2,5) (3,6)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Does the table $\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&0&1&2&3\\\hline y&3&4&5&6\end{array}$ describe the same relationship as the graph?
foundation.solution_text:
$(0,3),(1,4),(2,5),(3,6)$
Yes; every column is a marked point on the line.

foundation.question_text: A pattern begins at $4$ when $x=1$ and increases by $3$ each time $x$ increases by $1$. Which table describes the same pattern: $A=\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&1&2&3&4\\\hline y&4&7&10&13\end{array}$ or $B=\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&1&2&3&4\\\hline y&4&8&12&16\end{array}$?

development.question_text: Which coordinate set describes the equation $y=3x+1$: $A=\{(0,1),(1,4),(2,7)\}$ or $B=\{(0,1),(1,3),(2,5)\}$?

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-3,ymax=7,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={-2,0,2,4,6},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.3:4.4,samples=2] {2*x-2};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,-2) (1,0) (2,2) (3,4) (4,6)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The equation is $y=2x-2$ and its graph is shown. In the list $(0,-2),(1,0),(2,3),(3,4),(4,6)$, one coordinate does not belong. Which one?
development.solution_text:
$x=2:\ 2(2)-2=2$
$(2,3)\ne(2,2)$
$(2,3)$ does not belong; the graph gives $(2,2)$.

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=4,ymin=-1,ymax=7,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4},ytick={0,2,4,6},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.3:3.4,samples=2] {-2*x+6};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,6) (1,4) (2,2) (3,0)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph passes through the marked points. Which equation matches it: $y=-2x+6$ or $y=-x+6$?

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=4,ymin=-1,ymax=10,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4},ytick={0,2,4,6,8,10},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.3:3.4,samples=2] {2*x+3};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,3) (1,5) (2,7) (3,9)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Which table matches the displayed graph: $A=\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&0&1&2&3\\\hline y&3&5&7&9\end{array}$ or $B=\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&0&1&2&3\\\hline y&3&4&5&6\end{array}$?

[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=4,ymin=-3,ymax=8,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4},ytick={-2,0,2,4,6,8},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.3:3.6,samples=2] {3*x-2};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,-2) (1,1) (2,4) (3,7)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The pattern gives values $1,4,7$ at $x=1,2,3$; the equation is $y=3x-2$; the table is $\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&0&1&2&3\\\hline y&-2&1&4&7\end{array}$; and the graph is shown. Explain why all four describe the same relationship.
development.solution_text:
$x=1:\ y=1;\quad x=3:\ y=7$
The columns $(0,-2),(1,1),(2,4),(3,7)$ satisfy $y=3x-2$ and lie on the line, and the pattern and graph give the same $y$ at each shared $x$.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=6,ymin=-1,ymax=6,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5,6},ytick={0,1,2,3,4,5,6},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.5:5.5,samples=2] {-x+5};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,5) (1,4) (2,3) (3,2) (4,1)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
A learner says the table $\begin{array}{c|cccc}x&0&1&2&3\\\hline y&5&4&3&2\end{array}$ matches the equation $y=-2x+5$ because both give $(0,5)$. The graph shows the table's relationship. Explain the error and state the equation that actually matches.
mastery.solution_text:
$x=1:\ -2(1)+5=3$
$3\ne4$
One shared point is not enough. At $x=1$ the table and graph give $y=4$, but $y=-2x+5$ gives $3$.
$y=-x+5$

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=5.4cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-1,ymax=10,xtick={-1,0,1,2,3,4,5},ytick={0,2,4,6,8,10},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={font=\small,/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,<->,domain=-0.7:4.5,samples=2] {2*x+1};\addplot[only marks,mark=*,mark size=1.8pt] coordinates {(0,1) (1,3) (2,5) (3,7) (4,9)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
Four representations are claimed to describe one relationship: the pattern values $3,5,7,10$ at $x=1,2,3,4$; the equation $y=2x+1$; the table $\begin{array}{c|ccccc}x&0&1&2&3&4\\\hline y&1&3&5&7&9\end{array}$; and the graph. One representation is inconsistent. Identify it and justify using two $x$-values.
mastery.solution_text:
$x=3:\ y=7;\quad x=4:\ y=9$
At $x=3$ every representation gives $y=7$, but at $x=4$ the equation, table and graph give $y=9$ while the pattern lists $10$.
The pattern is inconsistent; at $x=4$ it should be $9$, not $10$.
